title: Quiz: Combinatorial Optimization Problems
description: Test your understanding of problem formulations for QAOA

questions.question: What does QUBO stand for?
questions.explanation: QUBO stands for Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization, which involves minimizing a quadratic function over binary variables.

questions.question: In the MaxCut Hamiltonian C = Σ (1 - Z_i Z_j)/2, when does an edge contribute to the cost?

options.text: When both qubits are in state |0⟩
When both qubits are in state |1⟩
When the qubits are in different states
When exactly one qubit is measured

questions.explanation: The term (1 - Z_i Z_j)/2 equals 1 when the qubits have different values (one |0⟩, one |1⟩) and 0 when they have the same value.

questions.question: How are constraints typically handled in QAOA?

options.text: Using penalty terms added to the cost function
By reducing the number of qubits

questions.explanation: Constraints are typically encoded as penalty terms that increase the cost when violated, encouraging the optimizer to find feasible solutions.

questions.question: The Traveling Salesman Problem requires n qubits for n cities.
questions.explanation: TSP typically requires n² qubits using one-hot encoding: one binary variable x_{i,t} for whether city i is visited at time t.

questions.question: What is the relationship between QUBO variables (z ∈ {0,1}) and Ising spins (s ∈ {-1,+1})?
questions.explanation: The conversion is z = (1 - s)/2, which maps s = +1 to z = 0 and s = -1 to z = 1.

questions.question: In portfolio optimization, what does the risk aversion parameter control?

options.text: The number of assets to include
The trade-off between expected return and variance

questions.explanation: Risk aversion (λ) controls the trade-off between maximizing expected return and minimizing portfolio variance: max(return - λ × variance).

questions.question: What happens if penalty weights for constraints are too small?

options.text: The optimal solution is always found

questions.explanation: If penalty weights are too small, the cost reduction from violating constraints may outweigh the penalty, leading to infeasible solutions.

questions.question: Which problems can be naturally formulated for QAOA? (Select all)
questions.explanation: QAOA is designed for discrete combinatorial problems like MaxCut (a), SAT (b), and graph coloring (c). Continuous optimization (d) requires different approaches.
